front: Figure: nicknamed the "Lion of the North" for his military success in the Thirty Years' War
Figure: Protestant King of Sweden of the House of Vasa who led his country into the Thirty Years' War on behalf of the Lutherans
Figure: relied on his chancellor Axel Oxenstierna, who continued directing the war effort after his death
Figure: founded the city of Gothenburg
Battle: Swedish troops outflanked the traditionally arranged tercios of the Count of Tilly at this 1631 engagement near Leipzig
Figure: personally led the Hakkapeliitta cavalry into the fighting at the Battle of Breitenfeld
Battle: this 1632 engagement, fought a year after Breitenfeld, saw this king killed while fighting the Imperial cavalry of Wallenstein
Figure: entered his final battle without armor, declaring "Let the Lord God be my armor!"; was fatally shot after being dragged by his horse
Figure: following this king's death, Wallenstein was charged with treason for refusing to attack the now-leaderless Swedish army
Figure: won a 1632 battle after constructing a pontoon bridge over the Lech River
Battle: this king's nimble light artillery fatally wounded the Count of Tilly after the Hakkapeliitta crossed the Lech River
back: Battle of the Lech / Rain (Gustavus Adolphus)

front: Figure: this king's most storied campaign began with the defense of Stralsund and reached its high water mark at the Battle of the Lech
Treaty: this 1629 agreement with Poland gave the King of Sweden control of Livonia, ending his war against Sigismund III
work: Polish War & the Treaty of Altmark

front: Figure: suffered a defeat at the Battle of Trzciana during his Polish campaign, where his experimental leather cannons were captured
work: Polish War & the Treaty of Altmark

front: Figure: gained the fortress of Noteborg via the Treaty of Stolbovo, ending his war against Russia
work: Early Wars: the Kalmar and Ingrian Conflicts

front: Figure: nearly drowned at the Battle of Vittsjo, and after losing this war was forced to pay a large indemnity at the Peace of Knared
work: Early Wars: the Kalmar and Ingrian Conflicts

front: Figure: introduced the allotment system of conscripting soldiers by district
Figure: instituted new parish regulations, making it easier to tax and conscript his subjects
Figure: boosted his treasury with revenue from the Falun Copper Mine
Figure: deployed the Hakkapeliitta, lightly armored cavalry named for their battle cry
Figure: after his experimental leather cannons failed, lightened his artillery carriages and placed them under Lennart Torstensson's command
Figure: reintroduced linear tactics and shock cavalry, developments later cited by historian Michael Roberts as the start of the "Military Revolution"
Figure: promoted the Scotsman Alexander Leslie to Field Marshal; his army included the Scottish Mackay's Regiment and General Johan Baner

Figure: this king's wife suffered severe postpartum depression, reportedly attempting to drop their newborn daughter down a flight of stairs, and after his death forced that daughter to sleep beneath a casket containing his heart
Figure: his daughter succeeded him as monarch upon his death
Figure: this king is honored in Sweden each year on November 6 with celebrations including pastries topped with chocolate or marzipan reliefs of him
